metadata.description: Golden set for RAGAS evaluation of FastAPI RAG system

questions.question: What is FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: FastAPI is a modern, fast web framework for building APIs with Python based on standard Python type hints. It is one of the fastest Python frameworks available and provides automatic API documentation.

questions.question: What decorator do you use to create a GET route in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: You use the @app.get() decorator to create a GET route in FastAPI. For example: @app.get('/items') def read_items(): return items

questions.question: How do you install FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: You can install FastAPI using pip: pip install fastapi. For production use, also install an ASGI server: pip install uvicorn

questions.question: How do you run a FastAPI application?
questions.expected_answer: You run a FastAPI application using uvicorn. The command is: uvicorn main:app --reload. The --reload flag enables auto-reload on code changes during development.

questions.question: What is the URL to access the automatic interactive API documentation in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: FastAPI automatically generates interactive API documentation at /docs (Swagger UI) and an alternative at /redoc (ReDoc). You can access them at http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs and http://127.0.0.1:8000/redoc

questions.question: How do you declare path parameters in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: Path parameters are declared directly in the route path using curly braces and as function parameters with type hints. For example: @app.get('/items/{item_id}') def read_item(item_id: int). FastAPI automatically validates and converts the type.

questions.question: What is a Pydantic model and why is it used in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: A Pydantic model is a class that inherits from BaseModel used to declare request body schemas. FastAPI uses Pydantic models to validate incoming request data, convert types automatically, and generate API documentation. You declare fields with type hints and FastAPI handles validation.

questions.question: How do you declare query parameters in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: Query parameters are declared as function parameters that are not part of the path. For example: @app.get('/items') def read_items(skip: int = 0, limit: int = 10). FastAPI automatically reads them from the URL query string. Optional parameters can use Optional type or a default value.

questions.question: How do you handle HTTP exceptions in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: FastAPI provides HTTPException to return HTTP error responses. You raise it with a status code and optional detail: raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail='Item not found'). You can also add custom headers to the exception response.

questions.question: What are background tasks in FastAPI and how do you use them?
questions.expected_answer: Background tasks allow you to run operations after returning a response, without making the client wait. You add a BackgroundTasks parameter to your route function and call add_task() with the function to run. For example: background_tasks.add_task(send_email, email, message)

questions.question: How do you add CORS middleware in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: You add CORS middleware using CORSMiddleware from fastapi.middleware.cors. Add it to the app with app.add_middleware() specifying allowed origins, methods, and headers. For development you can allow all origins with allow_origins=['*'].

questions.question: How do you return a specific HTTP status code in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: You can set the status code in the path operation decorator using the status_code parameter. For example: @app.post('/items', status_code=201). You can also use the status module: from fastapi import status and use status.HTTP_201_CREATED for readability.

questions.question: How do you receive form data in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: To receive form data you declare parameters with Form(). First install python-multipart: pip install python-multipart. Then use Form in your function: @app.post('/login') def login(username: str = Form(), password: str = Form()). Form data is encoded differently from JSON.

questions.question: What is the difference between @app.get and using APIRouter in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: APIRouter allows you to organize routes in separate files and include them in the main app with app.include_router(). It supports prefixes, tags, and dependencies at the router level. @app.get directly adds routes to the main application. APIRouter is recommended for larger applications to keep code organized.

questions.question: How does FastAPI dependency injection work?
questions.expected_answer: FastAPI dependency injection uses the Depends() function. You declare a dependency function and pass it as a default value using Depends. FastAPI calls the dependency function, caches the result per request, and injects it into your route function. Dependencies can have their own dependencies creating a dependency tree.

questions.question: What is response_model in FastAPI and what does it do?
questions.expected_answer: response_model is a parameter in path operation decorators that defines the schema for the response. FastAPI uses it to validate output data, filter out fields not in the model, and generate response documentation. It ensures sensitive fields like passwords are never accidentally returned even if present in the returned object.

questions.question: How do you handle file uploads in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: File uploads use UploadFile and File from fastapi. Declare the parameter as: file: UploadFile = File(). UploadFile provides async methods like read(), write(), and seek(). You can also use bytes type for small files. Install python-multipart to enable file upload support.

questions.question: What is the difference between async def and def route functions in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: async def route functions run as coroutines and are best for I/O bound operations like database queries or external API calls without blocking. def functions run in a separate thread pool. FastAPI handles both correctly. You should use async def when using await with async libraries, and def for synchronous code or blocking operations.

questions.question: How do you use yield in FastAPI dependencies?
questions.expected_answer: Dependencies with yield allow cleanup code to run after the response is sent. Code before yield runs before the route, the yielded value is injected, and code after yield runs as cleanup. This is used for database sessions: db = SessionLocal(); try: yield db; finally: db.close(). The cleanup runs even if an exception occurs.

questions.question: How do you add custom middleware in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: Custom middleware is added using the @app.middleware decorator with 'http' as argument. The middleware function receives a request and call_next function. You can modify the request, call call_next(request) to get the response, modify the response, and return it. You can also use app.add_middleware() with a class-based middleware.

questions.question: How do you implement OAuth2 password authentication with JWT tokens in FastAPI?
questions.expected_answer: OAuth2 with JWT requires OAuth2PasswordBearer for the token scheme, OAuth2PasswordRequestForm for login, and python-jose for JWT encoding. Create a /token endpoint that verifies credentials and returns a JWT token. Protect routes using a dependency that decodes the token and gets the current user. Install: pip install python-jose passlib

questions.question: How do you structure a large FastAPI application with multiple routers, database connection, and authentication?
questions.expected_answer: Use APIRouter in separate files for each feature area, include them in main.py with app.include_router(). Use dependency injection for database sessions with yield dependencies. Create an auth dependency that validates JWT tokens and inject it into protected routers. Organize into folders like routers/, models/, dependencies/, and schemas/.

questions.question: How do you write tests for a FastAPI application including endpoints that require authentication?
questions.expected_answer: Use TestClient from fastapi.testclient wrapping your app. For authenticated endpoints use dependency_overrides to replace auth dependencies with test versions that return a test user. You can also generate a real test token and pass it in the Authorization header. Use pytest for the test runner.

questions.question: What is the capital of France?
questions.expected_answer: NOT_IN_DOCS
questions.note: This question is completely out of scope. The system should refuse to answer.

questions.question: How do I reverse a linked list in Python?
questions.expected_answer: NOT_IN_DOCS
questions.note: This is a general Python/CS question not related to FastAPI docs. The system should refuse.
